text: Welcome to This is My Architecture. I'm Kurt and today I'm in New York City and I'm joined by
Zezar from Nextcaller. Welcome to the show, Zezar. Thank you. Please tell us about your business.
Sure. So Nextcaller is a phone fraud detection tool that takes in users phone calls, customers
who send us phone calls to an API and authenticates the majority of them and flags potentially
fraudulent or spoofed calls. You brought your architecture. Let's dive right into it. How does it work?
Sure. So a customer will send us information about a phone call that they receive in real time. The
phone calls usually use like the SIP protocol. That information gets JSON encoded and sent to us by the
customer through API Gateway. Is that the actual call or is that this is just metadata around the call?
It's actually metadata around the call. Okay. So that metadata gets sent through API Gateway along with
the customer's credentials like an API key. That information goes to a custom authorizer Lambda
which then looks up the customer's information in DynamoDB. It authenticates the customer and then
that gets sent that information gets sent to our main vera call function. That's what we call our product,
vera call. And that vera call function then makes a API call to a SageMaker endpoint that we have built
that will take that metadata and actually do a prediction. And based on the results of that prediction
and a custom rule engine we will then return that data with the score back to the customer. And that
score will say whether it's a spoof or fraudulent phone call. So if I understand that correctly,
it says all this happens during the call. That's right. So this has to be really quick. What kind of
turnaround time do you have on that? It only is about 110 milliseconds. Like that's about the average that we
have seen over the last couple of days actually. That is really quick. So that is how fast you make
a determination whether the call needs additional information. What happens to when you let's say
bring back data that says this is a fragile end call? Yeah, so we'll return a risk score back to the
customer from 0 to 100. And if it's a 100 or anything above a 50 for example, then that means the phone
call is potentially risky. And the customer can then decide to get their call center to add more
authentication steps for that user because that user is probably doing trying to spoof a phone number or
they probably have some other fraud indicator. So then they can do further authentication on the customer.
That's great. So it sounds a lot of the business logic happens down here in the machine learning and
in that Lambda layer. Let's zoom in on that. What happens here? Yeah, so SageMaker is an Amazon product
that has many parts to it. There's a inference engine, there's a SageMaker notebook that uses Jupyter,
and there's also a training service. So we start with our data scientists and data analysts actually start
in the notebook. They take in this metadata. So I should add that when the customer sends this metadata,
it also gets stored into an encrypted S3 bucket through SQS. So that data actually goes to an
encrypted S3 bucket for every customer. So then that data is then available to SageMaker. So our data
scientists actually determine which of the indicators in that metadata are, which of the features are more
determinative of possible spoof or fraud. And they train models around that using MS, using SageMaker's
training jobs. Once we have like a model that looks good enough that predicts spoof or fraud,
together with our rules engine, we can then update and say that that model is a potential candidate for
production. So with every phone call, basically the model gets better. That's right. Because you're
feeding the data back, you're retraining the model, and you're making that determination. That's right.
So what's next? So what we're actually currently working on is a way to make this process right here
of the retraining to be absolutely automatic. So right now it's a little bit of a manual process
where we take the phone calls after a few weeks and then we retrain the model and we do that. We do
the testing manually, but we're working on a CI-CD type flow where we can actually automatically run
the latest version of our rule engine with the latest version of the models
with a set of phone calls that we know are either fraudulent or not fraudulent,
and then see how the model does in predicting them or how that combined
engine does in predicting them. And then we can then update the model in real time or say, for example,
oh, this model is performing worse. Let's send an SNS notification saying that we need to look deeper into this.
Okay, great. So this is a completely serverless architecture, yet it has a very fast turnaround time
between the call and the decision made on the machine learning layer. Yet with every call you learn
new things about the caller and you make your model better. This is great.
Thank you so much for sharing this with us and thank you for watching.
guys
